I never give investment, legal or job=C2=A0advice around here, but I did on=
ce write:Maybe the easiest lucrative job in finance is:=09Take a job at a h=
edge fund.=09Get handed an employment agreement on the first day that says =
=E2=80=9Cyou agree not to disclose any of our secrets unless required by la=
w.=E2=80=9D=09Sign.=09Take the agreement home with you.=09Circle that sente=
nce in red marker, write =E2=80=9C$$$$$!!!!!=E2=80=9D next to it and send i=
t to the SEC.=09The SEC extracts a $10 million fine.=09They give you $3 mil=
lion.=09You can keep your job! Why not; it=E2=80=99s illegal to retaliate a=
gainst whistleblowers.=09Or, you know, get a new one and do it again.And I =
am kind of proud of that trade? The theory here is that the US Securities a=
nd Exchange Commission has a whistleblower protection rule that says that =
=E2=80=9Cno person may take any action to impede an individual from communi=
cating directly with the Commission staff about a possible securities law v=
iolation, including enforcing, or threatening to enforce, a confidentiality=
 agreement.=E2=80=9D The SEC interprets this rule in a somewhat extreme way=
: It thinks that even having a confidentiality agreement counts as =E2=80=
=9Cthreatening to enforce=E2=80=9D the agreement. If your company asks you =
to sign a nondisclosure=C2=A0agreement saying =E2=80=9Cyou agree not to dis=
close any of our secrets unless required by law,=E2=80=9D and you sign, and=
 then you discover some secret securities fraud at the company and want to =
report it to the SEC, you might think =E2=80=9Cbut wait: I signed an NDA sa=
ying I wouldn=E2=80=99t disclose any secrets, and this is secret, and I am =
not sure I am=C2=A0required by law=C2=A0to disclose it to the SEC, so if I =
do disclose it the company can sue me, and that=E2=80=99s scary, so I won=
=E2=80=99t.=E2=80=9D So the company has successfully impeded you from going=
 to the SEC, by threatening you with the NDA.So instead the company=E2=80=
=99s NDA has to say, like, =E2=80=9Cyou agree not to disclose any of our se=
crets unless required by law, except that you can say absolutely anything y=
ou like to the SEC.=E2=80=9D (Needless to say this is not legal advice.)=C2=
=A0This is the SEC=E2=80=99s theory, and you don=E2=80=99t have to like it,=
 but it seems to work: The SEC has extracted several large fines from finan=
cial-services firms for having their employees sign NDAs that are insuffici=
ently encouraging about talking to the SEC. And of course, if the SEC learn=
s about misconduct from a whistleblower, and then extracts a large fine ove=
r that misconduct, it can kick back part of the fine to the whistleblower. =
So there is a potentially lucrative business in signing faulty NDAs and the=
n reporting them to the SEC.=C2=A0But there is nothing particularly financi=
al-services-y about this. The rule says that no=C2=A0person=C2=A0can threat=
en whistleblowers, not no=C2=A0financial firm. Any company that could=C2=A0=
possibly=C2=A0do securities fraud in the US =E2=80=94=C2=A0any company subj=
ect to US securities law =E2=80=94=C2=A0could have a whistleblower, and thu=
s could get in trouble for having NDAs that don=E2=80=99t specifically allo=
w talking to the SEC. So far the action seems to be mostly in financial ser=
vices, probably for some combination of reasons including (1) the SEC tends=
 to pay more attention to them and (2) the SEC tends to have more leverage =
over financial firms than other firms, so it=E2=80=99s easier to extract fi=
nes.I am tempted to say that a third reason is, like, =E2=80=9Cfinancial se=
rvices firms employ some number of Money Stuff readers, who either read abo=
ut this trade here or discovered it independently due to their shared inter=
est in this sort of thing, so the SEC gets more whistleblower complaints ab=
out financial-services NDAs than it does about ball-bearings-industry NDAs.=
=E2=80=9D But that=E2=80=99s probably not it.Anyway:OpenAI whistleblowers h=
ave filed a complaint with the Securities and Exchange Commission alleging =
the artificial intelligence company illegally prohibited its employees from=
 warning regulators about the grave risks its technology may pose to humani=
ty, calling for an investigation.The whistleblowers said OpenAI issued its =
employees overly restrictive employment, severance and nondisclosure agreem=
ents that could have led to penalties against workers who raised concerns a=
bout OpenAI to federal regulators, according to a seven-page letter sent to=
 the SEC commissioner earlier this month that referred to the formal compla=
int. The letter was obtained exclusively by The Washington Post.OpenAI made=
 staff sign employee agreements that required them to waive their federal r=
ights to whistleblower compensation, the letter said. These agreements also=
 required OpenAI staff to get prior consent from the company if they wished=
 to disclose information to federal authorities. OpenAI did not create exem=
ptions in its employee nondisparagement clauses for disclosing securities v=
iolations to the SEC.OpenAI is a private company, and it is not clear that =
=E2=80=9Cwe are building robots that could enslave humanity=E2=80=9D would =
actually be a violation of securities laws. But if you have learned anythin=
g around here I hope it is that (1) private companies are not allowed to do=
 securities fraud either and (2) everything is securities fraud, so surely =
building robots that might enslave humanity could be=C2=A0securities fraud.=
 So employees need to be able to report it. So go get that money, OpenAI wh=
istleblowers.

      Toxic flow
   =20
 =20
In general, you should try to trade with people who are bad at trading, and=
 avoid trading with people who are good at trading. If someone comes to you=
 with an unblemished track record of buying stocks before they go up and se=
lling stocks before they go down, and offers to sell you some stock, why wo=
uld you buy it? It will go down. This is called =E2=80=9Cadverse selection.=
=E2=80=9DMuch of market structure consists of:=09Market makers (people who =
buy and sell stock for a living) trying to identify people who are good at =
trading, so as to avoid trading with them, and=09People who are good at tra=
ding trying to disguise themselves, so that market makers will trade with t=
hem.I=E2=80=99m exaggerating slightly: What matters is not so much =E2=80=
=9Cpeople who are good at trading=E2=80=9D as it is =E2=80=9Cpeople whose t=
rades predict price moves.=E2=80=9D Sometimes this is because they are good=
 at trading: They buy stocks before they go up. Sometimes it=E2=80=99s beca=
use they have inside information.=C2=A0Sometimes it is because they are=C2=
=A0big:=C2=A0If you=E2=80=99re going to buy $100 million of stock, the pric=
e of that stock is going to go up in the short run, whether or not you are =
right in the long run.=C2=A0So we talk sometimes about the feature of US eq=
uity market structure called =E2=80=9Cpayment for order flow,=E2=80=9D in w=
hich market makers compete to trade with the customers of=C2=A0retail broke=
rage firms. The point here is that if you can get a supply of trades that a=
re=C2=A0only=C2=A0from a retail brokerage, then you know that those are all=
 retail trades. The retail customers=C2=A0probably=C2=A0aren=E2=80=99t huge=
, and they=C2=A0probably=C2=A0aren=E2=80=99t informed: These are not trades=
 from professional traders managing billions of dollars of client money; th=
ey=E2=80=99re individuals trading their personal accounts on their lunch br=
eaks. You can be more granular about this: We=C2=A0talked once about a stud=
y finding that market makers charge different prices to customers of differ=
ent retail brokerages, possibly because some retail brokerages=E2=80=99=C2=
=A0customers are smarter and bigger than others.Or we talked the other day =
about algorithmic trading in foreign exchange markets, where customers who =
want to buy a lot of some currency will have an algorithm split their order=
 into lots of smaller bits, because =E2=80=9Cbuy a few euros=E2=80=9D tips =
your hand less than =E2=80=9Cbuy 1 billion euros=E2=80=9D does.One element =
in all of this is that your order can be anonymous: If you want to buy stoc=
k (as a hedge fund or an individual), you go to your broker and say =E2=80=
=9Cbuy me 100 shares of XYZ,=E2=80=9D and your broker goes to the stock exc=
hange or a market maker and says =E2=80=9CI=E2=80=99d like to buy 100 share=
s of XYZ for a customer please,=E2=80=9D but the broker doesn=E2=80=99t=C2=
=A0say who the customer is. In the general case it would be a betrayal of c=
lient confidence for a broker to say =E2=80=9CI=E2=80=99d like to buy 100 s=
hares of XYZ for Warren Buffett,=E2=80=9D for instance, because that could =
give the counterparty too much information and move prices against the cust=
omer. The=C2=A0customer relationship=C2=A0and the pricing of the trade=C2=
=A0are separate: The broker knows the customer, but the market maker doing =
the trade does not.This is not always true: If you=E2=80=99re a hedge fund =
buying high-yield bonds, you might call your salesperson at a big bank and =
say =E2=80=9Cwhat price would you quote me on Bond XYZ,=E2=80=9D and she=E2=
=80=99ll quote you a price, knowing who you are.
  [1]
 (You just called her on the phone.) There, the customer relationship and t=
he market-making function are combined (usually), so the salesperson=C2=A0h=
as some information about how badly she=E2=80=99s about to be adversely sel=
ected. There are some hedge funds whose salespeople groan when the phone ri=
ngs: They are too good at trading, so trading with them is a good way to lo=
se money.Anyway here=E2=80=99s the Wall Street Journal on sports betting:So=
me gamblers who manage to beat sportsbooks say they are often shut down whe=
n they succeed too much.Dave Holmes, a sports bettor in Chicago, said that =
as he started to win more using a math-based wagering strategy, companies i=
ncluding BetMGM, ESPN Bet and Caesars began rejecting his bets. He typicall=
y puts down about $100 per bet. Some companies have offered instead to acce=
pt as little as 50 cents.=E2=80=9CI would love to actually be given an answ=
er to why it=E2=80=99s happening, and they refuse to do that,=E2=80=9D Holm=
es said.Because you win too much! Why would anyone want to bet with you, if=
 you=E2=80=99re always right? The sports book is the market maker (it sets =
the odds and takes the other side of your bets), and it has the customer re=
lationship (you enter your information on=C2=A0its website), and if you kee=
p winning it will not want to play with you.
  [2]
Sports-betting executives say bettors who are limited make up a fraction of=
 their customer base and tend to be professional gamblers=E2=80=94unlike mo=
st customers, who bet on sports for entertainment. Betting heavily with pro=
motions could get a customer limited, some companies said.=C2=A0Sportsbooks=
 deploy sophisticated risk-and-trading desks to monitor how money flows thr=
ough various bets, attempting to minimize losses while offering attractive =
odds for customers. The companies say they have to manage their risk to ope=
rate the business profitably.=C2=A0Bricks-and-mortar casinos have long main=
tained the option to block winning players, including at sportsbooks, and m=
any successful bettors have to adopt ways to disguise their bets so that ca=
sinos can=E2=80=99t track what they are doing. The legendary sports bettor =
Billy Walters famously employed teams of people to spread out around Las Ve=
gas and place bets for him.=C2=A0I will say, while much of market structure=
 in =E2=80=9Creal=E2=80=9D finance is built around trying to avoid adverse =
selection, I can=E2=80=99t think of that many examples of=C2=A0this simple =
approach of banning or limiting successful traders.
  [3]
=C2=A0There are a few. Some banks=E2=80=99 dark pools =E2=80=94=C2=A0stock =
trading platforms =E2=80=94=C2=A0exclude traders that are too good. (The te=
chnical term is =E2=80=9Ctoxic order flow.=E2=80=9D) Or we talked once=C2=
=A0about a foreign-exchange =E2=80=9Carbitrage=E2=80=9D trade that Three Ar=
rows Capital did in its early days, before it became a huge crypto hedge fu=
nd (and then a bankrupt crypto hedge fund). The arbitrage was:=09All the bi=
g banks started offering electronic FX trading platforms to their customers=
.=09A customer like 3AC could be on every bank=E2=80=99s platform, but the=
=C2=A0banks=C2=A0generally couldn=E2=80=99t join each others=E2=80=99 platf=
orms.=09So sometimes two banks would quote different prices =E2=80=94=C2=A0=
one bank would buy or sell Ruthenian marks at $1.01 / $1.02, while another =
would buy or sell at $1.03 / $1.04 =E2=80=94=C2=A0and they wouldn=E2=80=99t=
 know it, but 3AC would.=09So 3AC would buy marks from Bank 1 at $1.02 and =
simultaneously sell them to Bank 2 at $1.03, for an instant profit.I wrote:=
 =E2=80=9CIn modern finance arbitraging your banks against each other is co=
nsidered not so much =E2=80=98an arbitrage=E2=80=99 as it is =E2=80=98rude,=
=E2=80=99 and the main risk in this trade is that if you did it too often t=
he banks would stop trading with you.=E2=80=9D That seems to have happened =
to 3AC, so they went on to bigger and worse things.This is actually similar=
 to a common sports betting strategy:=C2=A0=E2=80=9CSome frequent gamblers =
search the odds on everything from National Football League and Major Leagu=
e Baseball games to table tennis and darts, seeking weaknesses in prices se=
t by sportsbooks and making advantageous wagers,=E2=80=9D says the Journal,=
 and the simplest =E2=80=9Cweakness=E2=80=9D is when two sportsbooks have v=
ery different odds on the same event. You buy the cheap one, sell the rich =
one and lock in a profit whatever happens in the game. That can get you kic=
ked off the sportsbooks too.

      Sequoia Stripe
   =20
 =20
Dan Primack at Axios reports:Sequoia Capital is offering its investors a wa=
y to cash out of Stripe, the payments giant it first backed nearly 14 years=
 ago, Axios has learned. ...Limited partners in venture capital funds are i=
ncreasingly desperate for liquidity, as companies like Stripe eschew IPOs. =
...Sequoia this morning emailed LPs in funds raised between 2009 and 2012, =
offering to buy up to $861 million of Stripe shares.The purchasers would be=
 other, more recent Sequoia Capital funds =E2=80=94 a process partially ena=
bled by the firm's 2021 restructuring. ...The price would be $27.51 per sha=
re, which is Stripe's most recent 409A mark and represents a $70 billion va=
luation. For context, Stripe was valued at $95 billion back in 2021, but by=
 last summer had slashed its worth to $50 billion. =E2=80=A6The $861 millio=
n represents only around 10% of Sequoia's total FMV of Stripe shares.For a =
venture capital firm like Sequoia, I suppose this makes sense: The LPs in y=
our 2009 (!) fund are impatient to get their money back, but Stripe still i=
sn=E2=80=99t public; meanwhile plenty of people would like to own Stripe, s=
o offering new investors the chance to invest in Stripe is attractive. It h=
as the standard challenges of any continuation fund =E2=80=94=C2=A0mainly, =
you have=C2=A0hopeless conflicts of interest in pricing=C2=A0the trade, sin=
ce every extra dollar paid by the new fund benefits the old fund =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0but I suppose you manage those in standard ways. (Give investors the =
choice, don=E2=80=99t be greedy on fees, try to be able to support the valu=
ation, and use an outside valuation =E2=80=94=C2=A0here, the company=E2=80=
=99s appraised 409A valuation.)For Stripe =E2=80=A6=C2=A0I guess this is go=
od? Like:=09It=E2=80=99s barely even a transfer of shares: The shares move =
from Sequoia to Sequoia; from Stripe=E2=80=99s=C2=A0perspective, its=C2=A0s=
hareholder base stays the same. Just a slightly different set of ultimate e=
conomic owners.=09It=E2=80=99s a transfer at a price Stripe=C2=A0controls: =
Instead of shares trading freely at whatever price willing buyers and selle=
rs agree on, the shares trade only at a price endorsed by Stripe.I do wonde=
r sometimes what=E2=80=99s to stop them from doing this forever. It seems m=
ore attractive to Stripe than going public, and if it=E2=80=99s good enough=
 for=C2=A0the investors then maybe Stripe never needs to go public.

      Stablecoins are not securities?
   =20
 =20
Did we already know this? I don=E2=80=99t know, I forget. My view is genera=
lly:=09Probably stablecoins are not securities under US law. When people ta=
lk about cryptocurrencies being securities, they normally talk about the US=
 Supreme Court=E2=80=99s Howey decision, which says that a =E2=80=9Csecurit=
y=E2=80=9D includes an =E2=80=9Cinvestment contract,=E2=80=9D which is =E2=
=80=9Can investment of money in a common enterprise with profits to come so=
lely from the efforts of others.=E2=80=9D A stablecoin is an investment of =
$1 in a crypto token with the expectation of getting $1 back, with no profi=
ts and no efforts.=09That said, I think there=E2=80=99s an argument that st=
ablecoins are securities, not because they are =E2=80=9Cinvestment contract=
s=E2=80=9D but because they are =E2=80=9Cnotes=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Cevidenc=
e of indebtedness,=E2=80=9D which also qualify as securities. But nobody el=
se seems to think this, so never mind.=09But an=C2=A0interest-bearing=C2=A0=
stablecoin probably is=C2=A0a security, or at least there=E2=80=99s a bette=
r argument that it is: There is an expectation of profit from the common en=
terprise (investing the the stablecoin=E2=80=99s assets in something that p=
ays interest). Most stablecoins=C2=A0don=E2=80=99t directly=C2=A0pay intere=
st, and I suspect this is part of the reason.
  [4]
=C2=A0And a couple of big traditional asset managers =E2=80=94=C2=A0BlackRo=
ck Inc. and Franklin Templeton =E2=80=94 have launched what people=C2=A0cal=
l =E2=80=9Ctokenized money market funds,=E2=80=9D=C2=A0which I think are cl=
early (1) stablecoins (2) that pay interest and (3) that are registered as =
securities with the SEC.Anyway though here=E2=80=99s some news:The Securiti=
es and Exchange Commission has ended its investigation into Paxos Trust in =
relation to Binance USD, a digital asset that Paxos issues and lists, the c=
ryptocurrency platform said Thursday.=C2=A0Paxos said it received a formal =
termination notice on Tuesday from the SEC, saying it won=E2=80=99t recomme=
nd taking an enforcement action against the firm over BUSD, a Binance-brand=
ed stablecoin pegged to the dollar on a one-to-one ratio. ...=E2=80=9CPaxos=
 Trust Company has always maintained that its USD-backed stablecoins are no=
t securities under federal securities laws and that the Wells Notice was un=
warranted and unjustified. We are proud of our relentless advocacy for stab=
le-value digital assets and that the SEC staff determined it will not bring=
 enforcement action against Paxos in connection with BUSD,=E2=80=9D Paxos s=
aid Thursday.=C2=A0The termination notice comes after a judge for the U.S. =
District Court for the District of Columbia in late June dismissed portions=
 of the SEC complaint against crypto exchange Binance while allowing others=
 to go forward. The SEC in June 2023 sued Binance, alleging the firm operat=
ed an illegal trading platform in the U.S. and misused customers=E2=80=99 f=
unds.=C2=A0Judge Amy Berman Jackson, in a memorandum on June 28, said that =
BUSD wasn=E2=80=99t a security.=C2=A0Here=E2=80=99s that opinion. It does s=
eem like life is going to get a lot harder for the SEC in crypto enforcemen=
t, and going after stablecoins hardly seems worth it.

   =20
 =20
Bloomberg=E2=80=99s Muyao Shen writes about Pump.fun, which sounds dumb:Sin=
ce its launch in January, more than one million memecoins have been created=
 on Pump.fun, a crypto project based on the Solana and Blast blockchains, a=
ccording to data compiled by Subin An, data analyst at crypto venture firm =
Hashed. The rising popularity of Pump.fun as the go-to place for retail inv=
estors to create and trade memecoins has brought over $50 million in estima=
ted revenue in just about six months, based on data aggregated by An and ca=
lculated by Bloomberg.Like memecoins themselves, the rise of Pump.fun has f=
aced criticism. Skeptics say the platform reinforces the perception of cryp=
to as a casino, distracting from the potential real use cases of the techno=
logy.Hahaha sure, all those real use cases. But there is a securities law p=
oint:Michael Selig, partner at law firm Willkie Farr & Gallagher LLP, said =
that the rise of memecoins is also in part =E2=80=9Cattributable=E2=80=9D t=
o the US Security and Exchange Commission=E2=80=99s approach to policing cr=
ypto markets. The SEC has been cracking down [on] the crypto industry and m=
ost recently, it sued crypto firm Consensys Software Inc. for failure to re=
gister as a brokerage and improperly having collected millions of dollars i=
n fees.=E2=80=9CMemecoins are seemingly the antithesis of investment contra=
ct securities,=E2=80=9D Selig explained. =E2=80=9CPeople buy memecoins to e=
xpress a viewpoint, be a part of a community, speculate on the attention va=
lue of cat pictures or for other entertainment reasons.=E2=80=9DThe SEC=E2=
=80=99s view is essentially that it is illegal to publicly offer a cryptocu=
rrency that represents=C2=A0an investment of money in a common enterprise w=
ith profits to come solely from the efforts of others. But an investment of=
 money in=C2=A0nothing, with profits to come solely from=C2=A0memes, is fin=
e. So you get the crypto projects that regulation allows. The SEC encourage=
s crypto to be dumb, and crypto obliges.

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">NDA$$$</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I never give investment, legal or job=
=C2=A0advice around here, but <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusines=
s.com/click/36065916.196401/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2F=
ydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMTAtMDMvdGhlLXNlYy1oYWQtYS1idXN5LXdlZWs_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDcxNT=
I0X01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX=
3Rlcm09MjQwNzE1JnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB4a=
f1fb4f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" st=
yle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-widt=
h: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgroun=
d-color: #ccc;">I did once write</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16=
px 0;">Maybe the easiest lucrative job in finance is:</p><ol style=3D"list-=
style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=09<li style=3D"m=
argin-bottom: 5px;">Take a job at a hedge fund.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-=
bottom: 5px;">Get handed an employment agreement on the first day that says=
 =E2=80=9Cyou agree not to disclose any of our secrets unless required by l=
aw.=E2=80=9D</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Sign.</li>=09<li styl=
e=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Take the agreement home with you.</li>=09<li styl=
e=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Circle that sentence in red marker, write =E2=80=
=9C$$$$$!!!!!=E2=80=9D next to it and send it to the SEC.</li>=09<li style=
=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The SEC extracts a $10 million fine.</li>=09<li st=
yle=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">They give you $3 million.</li>=09<li style=3D"m=
argin-bottom: 5px;">You can keep your job! Why not; it=E2=80=99s illegal to=
 retaliate against whistleblowers.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;"=
>Or, you know, get a new one and do it again.</li></ol></blockquote><p styl=
e=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And I am kind of proud of that trade? The theory here=
 is that the US Securities and Exchange Commission has a <a href=3D"https:/=
/link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/36065916.196401/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubGF3=
LmNvcm5lbGwuZWR1L2Nmci90ZXh0LzE3LzI0MC4yMUYtMTc/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB4c=
718c8e" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" st=
yle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-widt=
h: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgroun=
d-color: #ccc;">whistleblower protection rule</a> that says that =E2=80=9Cn=
o person may take any action to impede an individual from communicating dir=
ectly with the Commission staff about a possible securities law violation, =
including enforcing, or threatening to enforce, a confidentiality agreement=
.=E2=80=9D The SEC interprets this rule in a somewhat extreme way: It think=
s that even <em>having</em> a confidentiality agreement counts as =E2=80=9C=
threatening to enforce=E2=80=9D the agreement. If your company asks you to =
sign a nondisclosure=C2=A0agreement saying =E2=80=9Cyou agree not to disclo=
se any of our secrets unless required by law,=E2=80=9D and you sign, and th=
en you discover some secret securities fraud at the company and want to rep=
ort it to the SEC, you might think =E2=80=9Cbut wait: I signed an NDA sayin=
g I wouldn=E2=80=99t disclose any secrets, and this is secret, and I am not=
 sure I am=C2=A0<em>required by law=C2=A0</em>to disclose it to the SEC, so=
 if I do disclose it the company can sue me, and that=E2=80=99s scary, so I=
 won=E2=80=99t.=E2=80=9D So the company has successfully impeded you from g=
oing to the SEC, by threatening you with the NDA.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16=
px 0;">So instead the company=E2=80=99s NDA has to say, like, =E2=80=9Cyou =
agree not to disclose any of our secrets unless required by law, except tha=
t you can say absolutely anything you like to the SEC.=E2=80=9D (Needless t=
o say this is not legal advice.)=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This=
 is the SEC=E2=80=99s theory, and you don=E2=80=99t have to like it, but it=
 seems to work: The SEC has extracted <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomber=
gbusiness.com/click/36065916.196401/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGl=
uaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMTAtMDMvdGhlLXNlYy1oYWQtYS1idXN5LXdlZWs_Y21waWQ9Qk=
JEMDcxNTI0X01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0Z=
XImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjQwNzE1JnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a31=
9a23cC4af1fb4f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_b=
lank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bot=
tom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; b=
ackground-color: #ccc;">several</a> large <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloo=
mbergbusiness.com/click/36065916.196401/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9=
vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDEtMTcvbWFraW5nLWVzZy1hLWNyaW1lP2NtcGlkPUJCRD=
A3MTUyNF9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJ=
nV0bV90ZXJtPTI0MDcxNSZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a=
23cB529d0b89" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_bla=
nk" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-botto=
m-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; bac=
kground-color: #ccc;">fines</a> from financial-services firms for having th=
eir employees sign NDAs that are insufficiently encouraging about talking t=
o the SEC. And of course, if the SEC learns about misconduct from a whistle=
blower, and then extracts a large fine over that misconduct, it can kick ba=
ck part of the fine to the whistleblower. So there is a potentially lucrati=
ve business in signing faulty NDAs and then reporting them to the SEC.=C2=
=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But there is nothing particularly finan=
cial-services-y about this. The rule says that no=C2=A0<em>person=C2=A0</em=
>can threaten whistleblowers, not no=C2=A0<em>financial firm</em>. Any comp=
any that could=C2=A0<em>possibly=C2=A0</em>do securities fraud in the US =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0any company subject to US securities law =E2=80=94=C2=A0coul=
d have a whistleblower, and thus could get in trouble for having NDAs that =
don=E2=80=99t specifically allow talking to the SEC. So far the action seem=
s to be mostly in financial services, probably for some combination of reas=
ons including (1) the SEC tends to pay more attention to them and (2) the S=
EC tends to have more leverage over financial firms than other firms, so it=
=E2=80=99s easier to extract fines.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I am te=
mpted to say that a third reason is, like, =E2=80=9Cfinancial services firm=
s employ some number of Money Stuff readers, who either read about this tra=
de here or discovered it independently due to their shared interest in this=
 sort of thing, so the SEC gets more whistleblower complaints about financi=
al-services NDAs than it does about ball-bearings-industry NDAs.=E2=80=9D B=
ut that=E2=80=99s probably not it.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><a href=
=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/36065916.196401/aHR0cHM6L=
y93d3cud2FzaGluZ3RvbnBvc3QuY29tL3RlY2hub2xvZ3kvMjAyNC8wNy8xMy9vcGVuYWktc2Fm=
ZXR5LXJpc2tzLXdoaXN0bGVibG93ZXItc2VjLw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBb604d4a8" i=
temprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"co=
lor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; b=
order-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: =
#ccc;">Anyway</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">OpenAI whist=
leblowers have filed a complaint with the Securities and Exchange Commissio=
n alleging the artificial intelligence company illegally prohibited its emp=
loyees from warning regulators about the grave risks its technology may pos=
e to humanity, calling for an investigation.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;=
">The whistleblowers said OpenAI issued its employees overly restrictive em=
ployment, severance and nondisclosure agreements that could have led to pen=
alties against workers who raised concerns about OpenAI to federal regulato=
rs, according to a seven-page letter sent to the SEC commissioner earlier t=
his month that referred to the formal complaint. The letter was obtained ex=
clusively by The Washington Post.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">OpenAI ma=
de staff sign employee agreements that required them to waive their federal=
 rights to whistleblower compensation, the letter said. These agreements al=
so required OpenAI staff to get prior consent from the company if they wish=
ed to disclose information to federal authorities. OpenAI did not create ex=
emptions in its employee nondisparagement clauses for disclosing securities=
 violations to the SEC.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">OpenAI=
 is a private company, and it is not clear that =E2=80=9Cwe are building ro=
bots that could enslave humanity=E2=80=9D would actually be a violation of =
<em>securities</em> laws. But if you have learned anything around here I ho=
pe it is that (1) private companies are not allowed to do securities fraud =
either and (2) everything is securities fraud, so surely building robots th=
at might enslave humanity could be=C2=A0securities fraud. So employees need=
 to be able to report it. So go get that money, OpenAI whistleblowers.</p><=

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=

5px;">Toxic flow</h2>
</td>

</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In general, you should try to trade wi=
th people who are bad at trading, and avoid trading with people who are goo=
d at trading. If someone comes to you with an unblemished track record of b=
uying stocks before they go up and selling stocks before they go down, and =
offers to sell you some stock, why would you buy it? It will go down. This =
is called =E2=80=9Cadverse selection.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px =
0;">Much of market structure consists of:</p><ol style=3D"list-style-type: =
decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom=
: 5px;">Market makers (people who buy and sell stock for a living) trying t=
o identify people who are good at trading, so as to avoid trading with them=
, and</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">People who are good at tradi=
ng trying to disguise themselves, so that market makers will trade with the=
m.</li></ol><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I=E2=80=99m exaggerating slightly:=
 What matters is not so much =E2=80=9Cpeople who are good at trading=E2=80=
=9D as it is =E2=80=9Cpeople whose trades predict price moves.=E2=80=9D Som=
etimes this is because they are good at trading: They buy stocks before the=
y go up. Sometimes it=E2=80=99s because they have inside information.=C2=A0=
Sometimes it is because they are=C2=A0<em>big:=C2=A0</em>If you=E2=80=99re =
going to buy $100 million of stock, the price of that stock is going to go =
up in the short run, whether or not you are right in the long run.=C2=A0</p=
><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So we talk sometimes about the feature of US =
equity market structure called =E2=80=9C<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomb=

10px;">
<div id=3D"footnote-1" class=3D"footnote-content" style=3D"font-style: ital=
ic;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">[1] Ordinarily you=E2=80=99ll call her for a t=
wo-way quote =E2=80=94 =E2=80=9Cwhat price would you buy or sell Bond XYZ a=
t=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94 and she=E2=80=99ll quote you two prices =E2=80=94 =E2=
=80=9CI=E2=80=99d buy at $99 or sell at $99.50=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94 and then =
you=E2=80=99ll tell her which one you want. The point of this convention is=
 that she knows *who* you are, but not *which side* of the trade you are on=
. If you called her only for one-way quote =E2=80=94 =E2=80=9CI want to sel=
l Bond XYZ, how much will you give me for it?=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94 she would =
have too much information, and would quote you a low price if she knew that=
 your trades were predictive.</p>
</div>
<div id=3D"footnote-2" class=3D"footnote-content" style=3D"font-style: ital=
ic;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">[2] Though it might want to take your side of =
the bet. <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/36065916.=
196401/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cudGhlZGlmZi5jby9hcmNoaXZlL3RoZS1xdWFsaXR5LWZhY3Rvci8/=
607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB80b50125" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"item=
scope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !im=
portant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bot=
tom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Byrne Hobart</a>: =E2=80=9CWhat'=
s interesting is that they're still letting players gamble when they have a=
n edge. If someone controls the full stack, and can identify smarter player=
s, the optimal strategy is not to refuse to deal with them. Instead, it's t=
o give them just enough bet size to make their bets worthwhile, and then to=
 adjust pricing for everyone else accordingly.=E2=80=9D</p>
</div>
<div id=3D"footnote-3" class=3D"footnote-content" style=3D"font-style: ital=
ic;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">[3] Part of it is just that, in a lot of the c=
ontexts where you are calling a market maker for a quote, the market maker =
is giving you an individual quote rather than just letting you access a bet=
 that is available to anyone. So if the market maker knows that you are tro=
uble, she can give you a very wide quote, or offer to trade only a small si=
ze =E2=80=94 but it=E2=80=99s not like your =E2=80=9Caccount=E2=80=9D is =
=E2=80=9Climited=E2=80=9D in the same transparent way that the sportsbook w=
ould do it.</p>
</div>
<div id=3D"footnote-4" class=3D"footnote-content" style=3D"font-style: ital=
ic;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">[4] =E2=80=9CIt=E2=80=99s more profitable to k=
eep the interest=E2=80=9D is probably a bigger part of the reason.</p>
</div>
